macro_segments.title: Vessels Completed in Purity (Kelim Hanigmarim Betahara) and the Concern for Contamination from Common Folk

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Three Categories of Stringencies for Kodesh versus Terumah
The Problem of Supervision: If a Chaver Supervised, Why Immerse?
Reinterpretation: A Chaver Supervised, But We Worry About Tzinora (Droplets from the Impure)
When Did Contamination Occur: Before or After Completion?
Resolution: Contamination Occurred Before Completion, When Spit Still Wet After Completion
Tosafot's Difficulty: How Can One Have Am Ha'aretz in a Shop Making Pure Vessels?
Social Reality: Interaction with Am Ha'aretz in Other Domains Despite Purity Concerns
Why Immersion Without Waiting for Nightfall: It's Only a Remote Stringency

macro_segments.title: The Case of the Tube Cut for the Parah Adumah: Immersion Versus Getting It Intentionally Impure

micro_segments.title: Introducing the Dispute: Rabbi Eliezer Says Immerse; Rabbi Yehoshua Says Get It Impure First
The Historical Context: Undermining the Sadducees' Position on When the Parah Service Could Be Performed
The Gemara's Question: Normal Immersion of Vessels Is Not a Leniency, So How Does Rabbi Eliezer Undermine the Sadducees?
Rava's Resolution: Vessels for Parah Service Are Treated Like Rishon (First-Degree Contamination) Like Those Touching a Sheretz
Objection: If It's Like Sheretz Contamination, Why Don't We Need the Third and Seventh Day Sprinklings?
Revised Resolution: Vessels Are Treated as Day Seven After Contamination by Death—Needing Only Immersion
The Concern: Are We Creating a New Category That Never Existed Before?
Abaye's Answer: The Category of Day Seven Vessels Exists; We're Not Making Up Something Entirely New
The Distinction: A Vessel Can Become Mishkav Only if Designated for Sitting; a Tube Cannot
Conclusion: For Parah, Vessels Are Treated Stringently as Av Hatumah but Leniently Allowing Immediate Use After Immersion

macro_segments.title: The Vessel Unifies: All Contents Are Treated as One Unit for Kodesh but Not for Terumah

micro_segments.title: The Principle: A Vessel Containing Items for Kodesh Unites Them; Contaminating One Contaminates All
Source: The Verse About the Incense-Filled Spoon, Which Contains Many Individual Crystals Yet Is Called 'One'
Gemara's Challenge: Rabbi Akiva's Stringencies Are Rabbinic, Not Biblical, So How Can We Derive This from a Biblical Verse?
The Ashes of the Parah Are Not Kodesh, Yet Rabbi Akiva Applies the Unification Rule to Them—Proof It's Rabbinic
Reish Lakish's Definition: The Rabbinic Unification Applies Only to Leftover Portions That No Longer Need the Vessel
The Biblical Rule: A Vessel Unifies Only When It Halakhically Needs the Vessel and the Vessel Has an Interior
The Rabbinic Expansion: Even When No Longer Needed or Without an Interior, the Vessel Still Unifies
The Dispute: Is Our Mishnah Biblical or Entirely Rabbinic? Rabbi Chiyya Bar Abba Holds It Follows Rabbi Akiva (Fully Rabbinic)

macro_segments.title: The Fourth Degree of Impurity in Kodesh: A Stringency Unique to Sacred Offerings

micro_segments.title: The General Framework: Stages of Contamination from Ab Through Shlishi; Only Kodesh Goes to Revi'i
Visual Chart: How Food Degrades Through Derivations; People and Vessels Only Reach Rishon Status
The Distinction: Chullin Can Become Sheni; Terumah Can Become Shlishi; Only Kodesh Can Become Revi'i
Textual Source: The Verse 'Meat That Touches Anything Impure Shall Not Be Eaten' Establishes Kodesh Can Reach Shlishi
The Mechusar Kippurim: One Awaiting His Purification Offering—Effectively Shlishi, Can Eat Terumah but Not Kodesh
Deriving the Revi'i: From the Fact That Mechusar Kippurim (Shlishi-Level) Cannot Eat Kodesh, We Infer Kodesh Must Reach Revi'i
Kal Vachomer: If Shlishi Terumah Makes Kodesh Invalid, Then True Shlishi (Food) Must Make Kodesh Even More Invalid, Creating Revi'i
